data.title: U.S. to begin blockade of Iranian ports Monday, but not all Hormuz
data.description:
U.S. Central Command posted Sunday that it will begin enforcing a maritime blockade on all traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports starting Monday, April 13 at 10 a.m. ET, following a presidential directive.

The blockade will apply to vessels of all nations traveling to or from Iranian ports and coastal areas, including those along the Arabian Gulf and Gulf of Oman. CENTCOM said the measures will be implemented impartially.

U.S. forces will not restrict freedom of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz for vessels transiting to or from non-Iranian ports, it added.

CENTCOM said additional guidance will be issued to commercial shipping through formal notices ahead of the blockade’s start. Mariners operating in the region have been advised to monitor official broadcasts and maintain contact with U.S. naval forces.

The move marks a significant escalation in U.S. actions targeting Iran’s maritime activity, with potential implications for regional shipping and energy markets.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Northrop Grumman launches Cygnus XL cargo mission to space station
data.description:
Northrop Grumman (NOC) on Saturday said it launched its latest cargo mission to the International Space Station, sending a Cygnus XL spacecraft into orbit aboard a SpaceX (SPACE) Falcon 9 rocket from Cape Canaveral Space Force Station.

 

 

The mission, designated NG-24, is carrying nearly 11,000 pounds (4,989.52 kg) of supplies, research equipment and experiments for astronauts aboard the International Space Station. In addition to delivering cargo, the spacecraft is equipped to perform orbital re-boost maneuvers that help maintain the station’s altitude.

Cygnus XL is the latest version of the company’s cargo spacecraft, introduced in 2025 with roughly one-third more carrying capacity than earlier models. The vehicle is built using components manufactured in the United States, including avionics systems, propulsion tanks and solar arrays.

Since its first flight in 2013, the Cygnus program has delivered more than 158,000 pounds (71,667.59 kg) of cargo to the space station.

Each spacecraft in the series is named after a figure in spaceflight history. The NG-24 vehicle is named for Steven R. Nagel, a former U.S. Air Force pilot and NASA astronaut who flew four shuttle missions and commanded flights that contributed to scientific research in orbit.

The launch continues a series of commercial resupply missions that support ongoing operations aboard the space station.

data.title: Boon for bulls? CTAs set to deploy $45B into stocks this week
data.description:
A confluence of technical forces is lining up behind stocks this week, according to analysis from Neil Sethi of Sethi Associates drawing on Goldman Sachs data — and the setup may be more durable than it first appears.

On the flow side, Goldman models CTAs as buyers of roughly $45B in U.S. equities over the next week, with approximately $34B of that concentrated in the S&P (SPX) (SPY) (IVV) (VOO). That ranks as the second-largest buy estimate on record. The shift reflects the pivot in systematic positioning from net sellers to net buyers that has been building through the early weeks of Q2.

The more structurally significant development, however, may be what has happened to dealer gamma. Coming into 2025, dealer gamma was heavily concentrated above the market — a positioning that repeatedly capped rallies, as dealers were mechanically selling into upside strength to hedge their long call exposure. Goldman notes that this created a persistent ceiling effect through Q1.

That picture has now inverted. Dealer gamma has migrated below the current market level, with dealers now running short gamma to the upside. The spread between dealer gamma at up 5% versus down 5% has reached its widest level on record — meaning the asymmetry in how dealer hedging flows respond to price moves is historically extreme. Moves lower will attract dealer buying as they rebalance short put hedges; moves higher will trigger further buying as dealers chase delta on their short call books.

 

 

The same mechanical flows that capped the market in Q1 are now oriented to amplify it.Goldman's caveat — "all else equal" — is worth keeping in view. A macro shock sufficient to reprice volatility materially could unwind the gamma dynamics relatively quickly. But absent that catalyst, the structural backdrop for a sustained move higher is, by some measures, the most supportive it has ever been.

data.title: FTC is said to weigh deal with ad giants over alleged coordinated boycotts
data.description:
The Federal Trade Commission is in talks with several major advertising firms to settle an investigation into whether they breached antitrust rules by collectively steering ad spending away from certain platforms, including X, The Wall Street Journal report Sunday, citing people familiar with the discussions.

The probe, launched last year, sought information from large holding companies such as Publicis Groupe (PUBGY) (PGPEF), WPP (WPP) (WPPGF), Dentsu (DNTUF) (DNTUY), Havas (HVBVF) (HVNVT) and Horizon Media. Regulators have been examining whether agencies, alongside advocacy groups, coordinated efforts to withhold advertising dollars in ways that could limit competition.

Under a potential agreement, some firms would pledge not to guide client budgets away from platforms based on the political nature of content appearing there. Advertisers themselves would still retain discretion over where their ads run. The talks are ongoing and may not result in a final deal. Any resolution would likely come without an admission of wrongdoing.

The framework mirrors conditions imposed in a prior FTC agreement tied to Omnicom Group’s (OMC) acquisition of Interpublic Group, which restricted coordinated decisions to avoid publishers based on ideology.

The scrutiny touches on the broader rise of “brand safety,” a practice that gained momentum after major brands found their ads placed next to extremist or offensive material online. Platforms responded by introducing tools to help advertisers avoid such placements.

Tensions escalated after Elon Musk acquired Twitter in 2022, prompting many advertisers to pause spending over moderation concerns. The platform, now X, later sued an industry group and several companies, alleging an illegal boycott. A federal judge dismissed the case, ruling that choosing competing platforms did not amount to an antitrust violation.

data.title: Hungary's Orbán concedes defeat as Magyar surge ends 16-year rule
data.description:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán acknowledged defeat in Sunday’s election, ending a 16-year stretch in power after early results showed a decisive win for challenger Péter Magyar.

Speaking to supporters, Orbán said he had congratulated Magyar and pledged to continue serving the country from the opposition.

U.S. President Donald Trump had endorsed Orbán and sent Vice President JD Vance to Budapest to campaign alongside him last week.

With roughly two-thirds of votes counted, Magyar and his Tisza Party led by nearly 15 percentage points. The party captured districts long held by Orbán’s Fidesz, while turnout approached 80%, among the highest since Hungary’s post-communist transition.

Magyar, a former Fidesz insider who broke away in 2024, capitalized on voter frustration over a weak economy, inflation and corruption allegations. His campaign reached hundreds of towns, including traditional Fidesz strongholds, drawing large crowds with an anti-corruption message and promises to restore ties with the European Union.

Orbán, who maintained close relationships with figures like Donald Trump and Vladimir Putin, had framed the election around national security, arguing he could keep Hungary out of the war in Ukraine. He also pledged to use international alliances to protect the country’s interests.

Hungary’s economic struggles and tensions with the EU, including billions in withheld funds over rule-of-law concerns, weighed heavily on the campaign. Magyar warned that continued Orbán leadership risked further isolation, telling supporters before the vote that the country faced a choice over its future direction.

data.title: GSK bets big on cancer comeback with aggressive trial push
data.description:
GSK (GSK) is preparing a high-stakes push into oncology, lining up five late-stage trials at once for a promising cancer drug as new CEO Luke Miels seeks to reshape the company’s historically cautious approach, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

The British drugmaker, long criticized for a weak pipeline, is attempting to accelerate development of mocertatug rezetecan, or Mo-Rez, with three trials in ovarian cancer and two in endometrial cancer. The strategy marks a sharper, more aggressive shift compared with past leadership, which investors often viewed as overly conservative.

Miels, who took the helm after nearly a decade under Emma Walmsley, has already helped lift sentiment around the stock, which has climbed roughly 50% since his appointment was announced last September. Still, he has acknowledged the need for bolder decision-making, saying earlier this year that GSK must “have more scientific courage.”

The simultaneous trials, each potentially costing tens of millions of dollars, mirror tactics used by rivals like AstraZeneca, which has surged ahead in oncology since GSK stepped back from cancer research in 2015. GSK re-entered the field through acquisitions and has since focused on gynecologic and blood cancers.

The approach carries risk. Large-scale late-stage trials can backfire, as seen with Sanofi, where disappointing results triggered a sharp stock decline and leadership changes. But some analysts say the shift could be exactly what investors want.

Early results for Mo-Rez are encouraging. Data presented at a recent oncology conference showed response rates of 62% in ovarian cancer patients resistant to certain chemotherapy and 67% in endometrial cancer patients, meaning tumors shrank or disappeared in those cases.

Mo-Rez, an antibody-drug conjugate designed to deliver cancer-killing agents directly to tumors, was licensed from China’s Hansoh Pharma in 2023. GSK is now leading its global development and views it as a potential blockbuster.

data.title: Trump says fuel prices may stay elevated as Iran conflict weighs on economy
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump acknowledged that oil and gasoline prices could remain elevated through the fall, signaling potential political and economic consequences from the ongoing conflict with Iran.

"It could be, or the same, or maybe a little bit higher, but it should be around the same," Trump said Sunday in an interview with Fox News’ Sunday Morning Futures With Maria Bartiromo when asked about the outlook for energy costs ahead of the midterm elections.

Gasoline prices have already climbed above $4 a gallon across much of April, according to industry data, underscoring the strain on consumers as the war disrupts global energy markets. The president’s latest comments mark a shift from earlier assurances that the price surge would be temporary.

Earlier in the day, Trump announced plans for a U.S. naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz following unsuccessful peace talks with Iran over the weekend.

"No one who pays an illegal toll will have safe passage on the high seas," he wrote on Truth Social.

The proposed blockade adds uncertainty to an already fragile ceasefire and comes after Iran restricted access to the critical shipping route, sending global oil prices sharply higher.

The conflict, which began in late February with coordinated U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran, has since expanded across the region, disrupting markets and raising geopolitical tensions. The fallout has also affected domestic politics, with public opinion surveys showing declining support for the war amid rising fuel costs.

Concerns are growing among Republicans that prolonged economic pressure could weigh on the party’s prospects in the upcoming midterm elections. Some lawmakers have also questioned the effectiveness of the blockade strategy.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Aussie dollar slide signals potential selloff in U.S. stocks after Trump Hormuz move
data.description:
Early moves in currency markets are pointing to a risk-off tone ahead of the U.S. trading session, following President Donald Trump’s announcement of a naval blockade of the Strait of Hormuz and the collapse of U.S.-Iran peace talks.

The Australian dollar, often viewed as a proxy for global risk appetite, dropped sharply in early trading Monday, offering a potential preview of how equities may open.

“AUD extending losses in early trade to around 0.8% now…back of the envelope maths extrapolating that points to spoos down 1.4-1.6% at the globex open in 3hrs time…” Michael Brown, a foreign exchange strategist at Australian brokerage Pepperstone, in a post on X.

The relationship between the Australian dollar and S&P 500 futures (SPX), commonly referred to as “spoos,” is closely watched by traders as a real-time gauge of market sentiment. The currency tends to weaken when investors pull back from risk, often preceding declines in equity futures.

Brown’s estimate suggests that the selloff in the aussie could translate into a notable drop in U.S. stock futures when trading resumes, reflecting heightened geopolitical anxiety.

Markets are reacting to a rapidly escalating situation in the Middle East. Trump’s blockade threat targets one of the world’s most critical energy chokepoints, while the failure of diplomatic talks with Iran has raised fears of further disruption to oil flows and global trade.

If the correlation holds, the currency move may be an early signal of broader market stress, with equities poised to follow as investors digest the implications of rising geopolitical risk.

data.title: SiteOne rated Neutral in new research coverage at UBS
data.description:
SiteOne Landscape Supply (SITE) is entering 2026 with a solid long-term growth narrative, but near-term pressures tied to housing weakness and rising costs are likely to keep the stock range-bound, according to an initiation report from UBS.

UBS on Friday started coverage on the landscape supply distributor with a Neutral rating and a $140 price target, implying only modest upside from current levels. While the company remains a dominant player in a fragmented industry, analysts led by Matthew Johnson expect a challenging backdrop over the next several quarters.

Housing slowdown weighs on demand

The core issue facing SiteOne (SITE) is a lagged effect from declining housing starts. Builders sharply reduced activity in the second half of 2025, with single-family starts down roughly 7% year over year entering 2026. That slowdown is expected to ripple through completions in the first half of the year, directly impacting SiteOne’s (SITE) construction-related business.

Residential construction accounts for about one-fifth of the company’s revenue, while another roughly quarter comes from repair and upgrade activity. Both segments are under pressure as affordability remains strained and consumer confidence sits near historically low levels.

UBS expects organic daily volumes to decline modestly in early 2026, highlighting limited visibility for a near-term recovery.

Cost inflation adds another layer of pressure

At the same time, SiteOne (SITE) is facing a surge in input costs. Prices for key commodities have jumped sharply, including fertilizer, PVC and diesel, with increases ranging from roughly 55% to more than 75% year over year.

The company has responded with price increases and fuel surcharges, leveraging its historical ability to pass through costs. Still, UBS cautions that demand sensitivity to higher prices remains uncertain, particularly in a weak residential environment.

As a result, margin expansion is expected to stall in 2026, with UBS forecasting adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization margins of about 8.7%, slightly below the prior year and under consensus expectations.

Internal initiatives provide support, but not enough

SiteOne (SITE) continues to execute on internal improvement efforts, including a “focus branch” program aimed at boosting underperforming locations and expanding private-label products. These initiatives are expected to deliver incremental margin gains over time.

However, UBS believes those benefits will not fully offset macro headwinds in the near term. The firm expects only modest organic sales growth in 2026, with profitability pressured by both input inflation and elevated operating costs.

Long-term story remains intact

Despite near-term challenges, UBS maintains a constructive long-term view. SiteOne (SITE) is the largest wholesale distributor of landscape supplies in North America, operating hundreds of branches and continuing to consolidate a highly fragmented market.

Over time, expansion into categories such as hardscapes and growth in private-label offerings could drive margin improvement and market share gains. The company has also demonstrated consistent outperformance relative to peers in recent years.

Valuation reflects balanced risk

UBS values the stock at roughly 14 times forward enterprise value to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization, broadly in line with peers. At current levels, analysts see a roughly balanced risk-reward profile, with limited catalysts to drive a breakout in either direction.

In short, SiteOne (SITE) appears positioned for steady long-term compounding, but investors may need to “sit tight” as macro pressures play out over the coming quarters.

data.title: Top global stories this week as geopolitical tensions ease
data.description:
US stock indexes ended the week higher supported by seemingly lower geopolitical tensions. 

US, Iran, and Israel agreed to a two-week ceasefire for negotiations. Iran will reopen the Strait of Hormuz for two weeks, with transit coordinated with its Armed Forces.

For the week, the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq (COMP:IND) rose 3.2% and 4.1%, while Dow (DJI) booked a 3 rise.

Across the Atlantic, the European equities (STOXX) ended the week 4% higher.

On the data front, Eurozone retail sales slipped slightly in February after no growth in January, in line with expectations. Meanwhile, business activity in March was revised up marginally but still slowed from the previous month, marking the weakest expansion in the private sector in several months.

The UK Services PMI for March was revised down from its initial estimate and fell notably from the previous month.

In the week, London’s FTSE 100 (UKX) equities rose 2.3% while Germany (DAX:IND) and France (CAC:IND) markets gained 3.7% and 4.7%, respectively.

The Asia-Pacific markets 

In the last week, Chinese markets (SHCOMP) climbed 1.5%, while Japan’s Nikkei 225 (NKY:IND) rose 6.8%.

China’s annual inflation eased in March from the previous month, coming in below market expectations.

Japan’s producer prices rose at a faster pace in March, reaching their highest annual increase in several months and surpassing expectations, as ongoing cost pressures continued to push prices upward. 

Major Asian corporate news from this week.

  • Niu Technologies (NIU) reported a strong rise in first-quarter sales, with overall volume growing significantly year over year, driven by particularly robust demand in the China market.
  • Toyota (TM) has pulled ahead of BYD in Japan’s EV market, supported by government subsidies and strong demand for its updated bZ4X SUV.

ETFs to track Europe: ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)
